text: Hermann Ebbinghaus, testing his own memory in the 1880s with lists of nonsense syllables, discovered what he called the spacing effect: information reviewed at expanding intervals is retained far longer than the same material crammed into a single session. His forgetting curve showed that memories decay rapidly at first, then level off — and that each review resets the curve, with subsequent decays growing shallower. The mechanism is partially explained by retrieval effort: a memory recalled just before it fades requires more cognitive work, and that effortful retrieval itself strengthens the neural trace in ways that easy recall cannot.
question: According to the passage, why does retrieval that occurs just before a memory fades produce stronger retention?

options.text: It allows the brain to reorganize information into long-term storage during sleep
It resets the forgetting curve so subsequent decays become steeper
The effortful retrieval required at that moment strengthens the neural trace more than easy recall does
It triggers the hippocampus to consolidate the memory before it is lost entirely
